Retention policy should be: 1) Establish regulatory requirements (GDPR = up to 3 years for contracts), 2) Set mailbox retention (Exchange retention policy), 3) Use legal hold when needed, 4) Backup for disaster recovery (not compliance). This balances privacy (deletion when retention expires) with compliance (access when required).

homelab.title: Implement Email Retention & eDiscovery
homelab.description: Set up retention policies and practice eDiscovery for compliance.
steps: Exchange admin center: Retention policies > Create policy
Set retention: 3 years for business emails, 1 year for personal
Create legal hold: Security & Compliance > eDiscovery > Create case
Add mailbox to hold: all emails preserved indefinitely
Delete email from user mailbox - confirm it's gone from Outlook
Use eDiscovery Search: search for keywords, date range, sender
Results show 'deleted' email is still recoverable
Export results to PST for review
Release hold when not needed - respects retention policy

questions.question: Your company is deciding between on-premises infrastructure and cloud (SaaS/IaaS). What are the key trade-offs you should evaluate?
options: Cloud is always cheaper
Evaluate: capital expenditure vs operational expenditure, control vs managed services, compliance requirements, scalability needs, vendor lock-in, security responsibility
On-premises is more secure - never use cloud
The choice doesn't matter - they're identical
questions.explanation: Trade-offs: CapEx (on-prem: buy hardware upfront) vs OpEx (cloud: pay-as-you-go). Control (on-prem: full control, more work) vs managed (cloud: less management, less control). Compliance (some orgs require on-prem due to data residency). Scalability (cloud scales instantly, on-prem needs planning). Vendor lock-in (cloud services are harder to migrate out of). Security responsibility (cloud: shared responsibility, on-prem: your responsibility). Each organization's needs differ.

homelab.title: Compare On-Prem vs Cloud Architecture
homelab.description: Build both on-prem and cloud setups, compare cost/complexity.
steps: On-prem: Set up server, backup, redundancy, cooling (estimate costs)
Cloud: Set up equivalent on Azure App Service, SQL Database, Backup Vault
Compare 3-year costs: on-prem (hardware + electricity + support) vs cloud (subscription)
Compare management: on-prem (patches, updates, monitoring) vs cloud (Azure handles most)
Scenario: traffic triples - on-prem needs new hardware (2 weeks), cloud scales in minutes
Scenario: move to new location - on-prem needs new site, cloud just sign new region

questions.question: Design an IT architecture for a growing startup (currently 50 employees, planning to 1000+ in 5 years). Consider infrastructure, security, user management, disaster recovery, and budget constraints.
questions.modelAnswer: Year 1-2 (50-150 employees): Cloud-first approach - minimal on-prem. Infrastructure: Microsoft 365 (teams, email, docs), Azure (if custom app needed), small local NAS for file backup. User Management: Azure AD (directory, SSO), Conditional Access. Security: MFA on all accounts, basic EDR/antivirus, email filtering. Budget: ~$200/employee/year. DR: geo-redundant backups. Year 3-4 (150-500 employees): Add complexity - hybrid. Infrastructure: keep cloud + add hybrid identity sync, on-prem Exchange if email compliance needed. Security: advance to Zero Trust, implement PAM (privileged access). Budget: ~$300-400/employee/year. Year 5+ (500-1000+): Enterprise. Infrastructure: multi-region Azure, on-prem datacenter for compliance, VPN/ExpressRoute for integration. Security: SIEM, threat intelligence, advanced analytics. Budget: ~$500+/employee/year. Cost growth is sub-linear due to economies of scale. Key: start simple, add automation as you grow. Avoid over-engineering early (technical debt vs business velocity tradeoff).

homelab.title: Architect Scalable Startup Infrastructure
homelab.description: Design phased growth from 50 to 1000 employees.
steps: Phase 1: Set up M365 tenant, basic Azure AD, Teams, 365 apps
Phase 1 security: MFA, basic Intune MDM
Phase 1 budget: estimate per-employee costs
Phase 2 (200 employees): Add hybrid identity (on-prem AD sync to Azure AD)
Phase 2 security: Conditional Access policies, advanced threat protection
Phase 3 (500 employees): On-prem infrastructure - file server, Exchange hybrid
Phase 3 security: PAM vault for admin accounts, EDR on all endpoints
Phase 4 (1000+ employees): Multi-region architecture, SIEM/SOC
Document: cost per phase, technical decisions, rationale

questions.question: A critical business system goes down, and your disaster recovery plan hasn't been tested in 2 years. What is the primary risk?
options: You have backups - recovery is guaranteed to work
Untested DR plans often fail when needed - RTO/RPO targets may not be met, data may be corrupt, recovery procedures may have errors
DR testing is nice but not critical
You should never document DR plans - it's a security liability
questions.explanation: Untested disaster recovery is worse than no DR - it gives false confidence. Common failures: 1) Backup corrupt, 2) Recovery takes longer than RTO target, 3) Restored data is stale (exceeds RPO), 4) Documentation is outdated, 5) Key personnel have left. Best practice: test DR quarterly, document results, update runbooks annually.

homelab.title: Implement & Test Disaster Recovery
homelab.description: Create DR plan, test failover, and document procedures.
steps: Backup setup: daily incremental + weekly full backup to separate location
DR Plan document: RTO (recovery time = 4 hours), RPO (data loss = 1 hour)
Test 1: Restore to test VM - measure actual time vs RTO target
If actual > RTO: identify bottleneck (network speed, VM provisioning, etc.)
Test 2: Failover critical app - verify it works on restored system
Test 3: Data integrity - spot-check key data is consistent
Document: actual times, issues encountered, resolutions
Quarterly retesting: ensure procedures stay current
